text: The video displays the features of perihepatitis, showing inflammation around the liver with visible changes in tissue appearance.
The video shows the liver with visible adhesions attached to its surface.
The falciform ligament is visible, appearing as a thin, membranous structure extending from the liver towards the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon inserts the telescope port, providing a clear view of the surgical field for the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the transfacial insertion of the epigastric port, positioning it in the abdominal wall.
The camera view shows the liver, with its smooth surface and reddish-brown color visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon enters the abdominal wall from the left of the falciform ligament, passing through it to approach the right hypochondrium area where the gallbladder is located.
The surgical field reveals numerous adhesions surrounding the gallbladder, indicative of inflammation due to colostrum pancreatitis, observed on the tenth day post-presentation.
The video shows intra-abdominal adhesions, which are fibrous bands of tissue visible within the abdominal cavity.
The camera view shows the anterior abdominal wall, providing a visual overview of the surgical field before proceeding with the cholecystectomy.
The gallbladder is visible in the right hypochondrium area, surrounded by intra-abdominal adhesions.
Two surgical ports are inserted into the abdominal wall, providing access to the surgical field for the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon has inserted two laparoscopic ports, one through the infraumbilical area and another through the epigastric region, providing access for the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a 10mm laparoscope through the umbilical telescopic port to visualize the surgical field, while manipulating a Maryland Dissector through the epigastric port to address intra-abdominal adhesions.
The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector through the laparoscopic ports to remove adhesions from the gallbladder area.
The surgeon removes the telescope from the umbilical port area, preparing to insert additional ports for further access and clearance of adhesions.
The surgeon reinserts the laparoscopic telescope through the epigastric port, providing a new angle for visualizing the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the umbilical port, which was initially inserted blindly at the start of the procedure.
The surgeon uses an instrument to pierce through the adhesions in the surgical field, creating a clear path for further dissection.
Under telescopic vision through the epigastric port, the surgeon adjusts the position of the port, slightly withdrawing it for optimal visualization.
The laparoscopic telescope is positioned through the epigastric port, providing a view of the surgical field, while a working instrument is utilized through the umbilical port.
The surgeon applies traction to manipulate the tissue, likely to improve visibility or facilitate further dissection.
The laparoscope is cleaned to improve visualization, and the surgeon prepares to demonstrate the various port positions used in the procedure.
The surgical field shows multiple adhesions with most of the ports inserted. The gallbladder, stomach, greater omentum, and liver are visible, along with the recently inserted fifth port.
A 5mm port is being inserted into the abdominal wall, providing access for laparoscopic instruments. The gallbladder and surrounding structures are visible in the surgical field.
The laparoscope is being cleaned to improve visualization of the surgical field.
The camera focuses on the stomach, clearly visible within the surgical field.
The left lobe of the liver is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the gallbladder, which is visible in the surgical field, while radiations are present in the surrounding area.
The surgeon observes the right lobe of the liver, noting the presence of perihepatitis and fibrous bands. Adhesions from post-pancreatitis are visible around the liver area.
The surgeon applies traction and mechanical force to manipulate the gallbladder, aiming to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon inserts a port into the abdominal cavity, preparing for laparoscopic access during the cholecystectomy procedure.

A 5mm instrument is used for traction, revealing the initial findings in the surgical field: the gallbladder, greater omentum, colon, and duodenum are adhered to the gallbladder and the Kellogg's area.
The surgeon uses the right hand through the working port to apply traction and carefully separate the adhered components from the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector and an Atraumatic Grasper to strip adhesions from the gallbladder, which are typical in the post-pancreatitis phase, especially due to gallstone pancreatitis.
The gallbladder is observed with a wall that does not appear thickened.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the gallbladder using atraumatic instruments, avoiding the use of cautery or electrosurgery to prevent damage to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses mechanical traction and dissection with atraumatic forceps and a Maryland dissector to strip the gallbladder from adhesions, carefully manipulating the tissue without electrosurgery.
The assistant holds the fundus of the gallbladder to provide adequate traction, facilitating the surgeon's dissection process.
The assistant holds the gallbladder, applying traction towards the patient's right shoulder, above the hepatic area, facilitating exposure for dissection.
The surgeon carefully continues the dissection of the gallbladder, using atraumatic forceps and Maryland dissection techniques, while mechanical traction is applied to maintain exposure.

The surgical field reveals excessive fat and adhesions obscuring the skeletal triangle and posterior fold. The colon, grittomentum, and duodenum are visibly adhered to the gallbladder neck and Hartmann's pouch.
The neck of the gallbladder is positioned medially to the common bile duct (CBD), with visible anatomical orientation in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses electrosurgery to dissect tissue around the gallbladder, with the assistant applying traction towards the patient's right shoulder to aid in exposure.
The surgeon carefully strips away adhesions from the gallbladder neck and Hartmann's pouch, taking care to avoid damaging nearby structures such as the colon, omentum, and duodenum.
The surgeon uses a 5mm suction cannula to assist in clearing the surgical field, likely removing fluids or debris from the area.
A 5mm reducer is visible on the top of the epigastric port during the procedure.

The surgeon utilizes the umbilical port for the laparoscopic camera, while the epigastric port and additional working ports of 5mm and 10mm are employed for surgical instruments during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon utilizes three 10 mm ports for the procedure, visible in the abdominal area.

The surgical setup includes one port at the umbilical area, another at the epigastric region, and one at the left paraumbilical area, along with two 5mm ports on the right side, following conventional placement for cholecystectomy.
The gallbladder lining is visible after dissection, revealing the anatomical structure and surrounding tissue.
The surgical field shows extensive adhesions, obscuring the view of the reverse sulcus, which is fused in this patient.
A gauze piece is used for dissection, applying pressure, and aiding suction during the procedure.
The surgeon applies traction to facilitate the dissection of Calot's triangle, proceeding to clip the cystic duct and cystic artery before removing the gallbladder.
The gallbladder is visible in the surgical field, with its surface exposed for further dissection.
The cystic duct is visible in the surgical field, allowing for identification and further dissection.
The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector to manipulate and dissect tissue around the gallbladder area, focusing on the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses a traumatic grasp to maintain dissection, while addressing oozing or slight bleeding with suction.
The cystic duct is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the probable location of the common bile duct (CBD) during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgical field shows an absence of the Rouvier's sulcus, which is not visible in the current view.
The surgeon identifies the culottes, a key anatomical landmark, during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The outline of the gallbladder is visible, showing its anatomical shape and position within the surgical field.
